skill.name: code-validation-sandbox
skill.description: Validates teaching code examples with layer-aware strategy selection, language detection, and actionable diagnostics for Python-focused chapter content.
skill.summary: Validate instructional code examples with context-aware checks and clear error reports.
skill.author: 92Bilal26

security_audit.summary: Static analysis found many command, filesystem, and network indicators. Most README and SKILL.md backtick detections are documentation examples, and several weak-crypto or C2 keyword matches are false positives from ordinary text. However, the scripts execute extracted chapter code on the host, evaluate generated context variables, and pipe remote installer scripts into shells, so the skill should not be published without remediation.

high_findings.title: Host Execution of Extracted Chapter Code
high_findings.description: The Python validator extracts code blocks from markdown and executes them with python3 and timeout on the host environment. This is expected for a validator, but it is dangerous for untrusted chapter content because arbitrary Python code can access local files and network resources outside the Docker sandbox.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The script directly runs extracted files with python3. The behavior is intentional validation, but the code source is user-controlled markdown content.

high_findings.title: Shell Eval of Metadata-Derived Context Variables
high_findings.description: The main validator evaluates context variables produced by detect-context.sh. The LAYER value can come from chapter-meta.yaml, so hostile metadata can inject shell syntax into the evaluated assignment.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The data flow from chapter metadata to here-doc output and then eval is visible in the scripts. No sanitization or whitelist is applied before eval.

high_findings.title: Remote Installer Scripts Piped to Shell
high_findings.description: The setup script downloads installer scripts from external URLs and pipes them directly to sh or bash inside the Docker container. This creates supply-chain risk and can modify the bind-mounted workspace if the fetched script or network path is compromised.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The pipe-to-shell patterns are explicit and use remote HTTPS endpoints. They run in a container, which reduces but does not remove risk because the workspace is bind-mounted.

medium_findings.title: Docker Sandbox Is Not Used by the Python Validator
medium_findings.description: The skill includes a Docker setup script, but validate-python.sh runs python3 directly instead of docker exec. Users may believe validation is isolated when the active Python path executes on the host.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The setup script creates a container, while the validator invokes host python3. This mismatch is clear from the scripts, though intended deployment workflow may vary.

medium_findings.title: Broad File Reads and Generated Output Writes
medium_findings.description: The detector reads markdown files under the supplied chapter path, and the validator writes extracted code files under the output directory. This is normal for validation, but it should be limited to trusted project paths.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file operations are directly present and necessary for the skill. The risk depends on caller-supplied paths and workspace permissions.

low_findings.title: Documentation Command Examples Flagged as Execution
low_findings.description: Many external command findings in README.md and SKILL.md are fenced examples that document how users may run the validator. They are not executed by the skill merely by being present in documentation.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged lines are markdown examples or usage instructions. They do not create an execution path unless a user intentionally runs them.

low_findings.title: Keyword Matches Without Malicious Semantics
low_findings.description: Several blocker keywords are ordinary words in documentation, such as Replaces, C2 tier labels, exit codes, and validation status text. No evidence found that these lines implement command and control behavior or weak cryptography.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The line context shows documentation labels and educational terminology. I did not find evidence of cryptographic implementation or C2 behavior at these locations.

low_findings.title: Fixed Environment Checks Use Safe Subprocess Arguments
low_findings.description: verify.py uses subprocess.run for fixed python3 and docker checks. These calls do not include user-controlled shell strings and are low risk.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The subprocess calls pass argument lists with hardcoded commands and shell is not enabled. The calls are environment probes rather than arbitrary execution.

dangerous_patterns.title: eval on Generated Shell Assignments
dangerous_patterns.description: Uses eval on context_vars from another script, which can convert untrusted metadata into shell execution.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The eval statement and metadata-derived context output are directly connected by the surrounding script logic.

dangerous_patterns.title: Runtime Execution of Extracted Code Blocks
dangerous_patterns.description: Runs extracted Python files with host python3 during validation.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The command invocation is explicit and acts on files extracted from chapter markdown.

dangerous_patterns.title: curl Pipe to Shell
dangerous_patterns.description: Downloads remote installers and executes them immediately in shell pipelines.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: Both commands match the pipe-to-shell pattern and fetch executable installer content from remote hosts.

content.value_statement: Instructional code examples often fail because validation ignores learning context. This skill selects validation depth by teaching layer and reports actionable Python diagnostics.
actual_capabilities: Detects teaching layer from chapter metadata and markdown content patterns.
Detects Python, Node.js, Rust, and Go indicators from files and keywords.
Extracts Python code blocks from markdown into validation output files.
Runs Python syntax and runtime checks with layer-aware severity handling.
Creates a persistent Docker container with Python, Node.js, and common tools.
Produces validation summaries with counts for total blocks and failures.
limitations: Python validation runs on the host, not inside the Docker container.
Node.js and Rust validators are referenced but not included in this skill.
Documented exact output matching is not fully implemented in the scripts.
Remote setup downloads installer scripts from external package sources.

use_cases.title: Chapter Code Review
use_cases.description: Validate Python examples in a teaching chapter before publication and identify blocking syntax or runtime errors.

use_cases.title: Learning Content QA
use_cases.description: Check whether beginner-focused examples match the expected validation depth for manual learning material.

use_cases.title: AI Example Verification
use_cases.description: Review before-and-after examples and confirm that validation strategy matches the claimed teaching layer.

prompt_templates.title: Validate One Chapter
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner check for a single Python-heavy chapter.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use code-validation-sandbox to validate the Python examples in this chapter path. Report syntax errors, runtime errors, and recommended fixes.

prompt_templates.title: Force a Teaching Layer
prompt_templates.scenario: Intermediate review when the layer is known.
prompt_templates.prompt: Validate this chapter as Layer 1 content. Treat syntax and runtime failures as publication blockers and summarize each required fix.

prompt_templates.title: Audit Validation Depth
prompt_templates.prompt: Analyze the chapter context, explain the detected layer and language, then run the matching validation depth and summarize gaps.

prompt_templates.title: Compare AI Examples
prompt_templates.prompt: Validate the before-and-after AI examples in this chapter. Check whether both versions run and whether claimed improvements need more evidence.

output_examples.input: A Layer 1 Python chapter with several markdown code blocks.
output_examples.output: The report identifies the detected layer, total Python blocks, one syntax failure, and a line-specific fix recommendation.

output_examples.input: A chapter with no Python blocks but language keywords in text.
output_examples.output: The report explains that no executable Python blocks were found and recommends manual review for non-Python examples.

output_examples.input: A Layer 2 chapter with baseline and optimized examples.
output_examples.output: The report checks both variants, notes whether outputs match, and flags unsupported performance claims for follow-up.

best_practices: Run the validator only on trusted project directories or inside an external sandbox.
Review detected layer and language before accepting validation results.
Inspect generated extracted files before rerunning failures with broader permissions.
anti_patterns: Do not run this skill on untrusted markdown because extracted Python executes on the host.
Do not assume Node.js or Rust validation works until those scripts are added.
Do not treat Docker setup as isolation for validate-python.sh without code changes.

faq.question: What does this skill validate?
faq.answer: It validates instructional code examples, especially Python code blocks in markdown chapters.

faq.question: Does it support Claude, Codex, and Claude Code?
faq.answer: Yes. The metadata lists Claude, Codex, and Claude Code as supported tools.

faq.question: Is the validation fully sandboxed?
faq.answer: No. The Python validator runs code on the host, so use a separate sandbox for untrusted content.

faq.question: Can it validate Node.js and Rust today?
faq.answer: It detects those languages, but the Node.js and Rust validation scripts are not included.

faq.question: What does layer-aware validation mean?
faq.answer: The skill changes validation depth based on whether content is beginner, collaboration, reusable, or integration focused.

faq.question: Why is publication not recommended yet?
faq.answer: The audit found host code execution, shell eval of metadata-derived values, and remote installer scripts piped to shells.
